narrative: As an Asian male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.70—well above the typical 3.21 average—your law school application stood out in several ways, including your middle-family-income background and full-time study plans. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 65.9% chance that you would fail it, tipping the decision toward rejection to minimize the risk of poor bar passage outcomes for the program. This prediction wasn't driven by your strengths like that strong GPA, but primarily by three key factors, ranked by their influence on your specific case. The most influential was your LSAT score of 34, which falls below the average of 36.7 and significantly pushed the model toward predicting failure, as higher scores are strongly linked to bar success in the data. Next, your Year 1 school decile ranking of 6—roughly average compared to the typical 5.71—added to that risk by not standing out as exceptionally competitive early on. Your middle family income bracket, while common, also contributed toward the failure prediction, likely because the model associates it with certain patterns in bar outcomes seen across similar students. In contrast to typical applicants who often have higher LSATs or stronger early deciles to offset other areas, your profile clustered more closely with those predicted to struggle on the bar. It's understandable to feel frustrated, especially with your academic strengths as an Asian male applicant where high achievement is often the norm. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it above the average, as that single change could dramatically shift future predictions. Strengthening early-grade deciles through additional coursework or focusing on income-agnostic factors like extracurriculars tied to bar prep might also help. Many students in your position reapply successfully after targeted improvements—reach out to admissions counselors for personalized advice on bolstering your bar readiness profile. Your solid foundation positions you well for a comeback with the right adjustments.
